expectations.text: JWT verification uses an established library (e.g., jsonwebtoken) with the algorithm explicitly pinned to HS256 — does not accept algorithms from the token header (no 'alg: none' / algorithm-confusion vulnerability)
expectations.evidence: Uses jsonwebtoken@^9.0.2 with explicit `algorithms: [...config.algorithms]` where algorithms is `readonly ['HS256']`. Includes a test 'rejects alg:none tokens'. Comment: 'HS256 only; rejects \'none\' and alg confusion.'

expectations.text: The Authorization header is parsed defensively — handles missing header, malformed header, missing 'Bearer ' prefix, multiple spaces, and case variants without crashing
expectations.evidence: extractBearerToken checks for undefined/empty, oversize header, missing 'Bearer ' prefix, then trims and validates with strict regex `/^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$/`. Uses req.header('authorization') (case-insensitive lookup). Note: does not explicitly normalize 'bearer' lowercase prefix variant — startsWith is case-sensitive against 'Bearer '. Still defensive (rejects rather than crashes).

expectations.text: Middleware emits 401 (or 403 where appropriate) with a generic message; the response body does not leak the underlying jwt.verify error string, the token, or any stack content
expectations.evidence: `res.status(401).json({ error: 'unauthorized', requestId })` — uniform generic response, comment explicitly states 'Do not echo the token, header, or detailed reason to the caller (avoid oracle behavior).'

expectations.text: Token contents (payload) are NEVER logged or echoed back to the client; logs use structured key/value with user identifier only after verification succeeds
expectations.evidence: AuthSuccessEvent log includes only requestId/subject/tokenId/sourceIp; AuthFailureEvent comment: 'Intentionally no token, no secret, no raw header.' authSuccess is emitted only after verifyAccessToken succeeds and req.user is set.

expectations.text: Verified-payload extraction is restricted to expected fields (e.g., sub/iss/exp); the middleware does not blindly attach the full decoded payload to req.user
expectations.evidence: Only sub, roles (filtered to strings), and jti are extracted into a frozen AuthenticatedUser object. Comment: 'Only expected, typed fields are extracted (Request Surface Minimization). The raw payload is never attached to req.'

expectations.text: Process startup or initialization fails fast / logs an error if JWT_SECRET is missing or empty — middleware does not silently degrade to 'always allow' or 'always deny by accident'
expectations.evidence: loadAuthConfig throws 'JWT_SECRET is missing or too short...' if secret is missing or < 32 bytes. Called at app startup: `const authConfig = loadAuthConfig(); // fail-fast on bad config`.

expectations.text: Type-safe shape: req.user (or augmented Request) has an explicit TypeScript type rather than 'any'
expectations.evidence: Explicit `AuthenticatedUser` interface with readonly id: string, roles: readonly string[], tokenId: string. Express.Request augmented via `declare global { namespace Express { interface Request { user?: AuthenticatedUser; } } }`.

expectations.text: Output includes a Securability Notes block listing SSEM attributes enforced, ASVS references, trust boundaries, dependencies with versions, and trade-offs
expectations.evidence: Has '## Securability Notes' section with Trust boundary handling (S6.3, S6.4), SSEM attributes enforced (all 9 listed), ASVS Mapping table (V9.1.1, V9.1.2, V9.1.3, V9.2.1, V9.2.2, V9.2.3, V8.3.1, V16.x), Dependency selection rationale with pinned versions, and Trade-offs and follow-ups section.

expectations.text: Token replay/expiry handling is explicit (jwt.verify enforces exp; clock-skew tolerance is set or noted as a trade-off)
expectations.evidence: `clockTolerance: config.clockToleranceSeconds` set to 5 in config. TokenExpiredError mapped to 'expired'; NotBeforeError mapped to 'not_yet_valid'. Trade-offs section notes revocation/jti denylist as follow-up (ASVS 7.4.1).

expectations.text: Middleware is exported as a function factory or pure function so it is unit-testable without spinning up an Express app
expectations.evidence: `createJwtAuthMiddleware(deps: JwtAuthDeps): RequestHandler` is a factory accepting injected config/logger/clock. Pure helpers `extractBearerToken` and `verifyAccessToken` are independently exported. Sample Jest tests exercise verifyAccessToken without Express.
